title: Cat Torn Nail with Exposed Quick: Triage and Treatment Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's nail bleeding? Learn how to manage a torn nail and exposed quick, when to see a vet, and why a photo helps with triage.
content_html:
What is a Torn Nail with an Exposed Quick?

A torn nail occurs when a cat’s claw gets snagged on furniture, carpet, or other surfaces, causing the outer shell of the nail to break or pull away. This often exposes the "quick," which is the central part of the claw containing sensitive nerves and blood vessels. Because the quick is highly vascularized, an injury here typically results in immediate bleeding and significant discomfort for your cat.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a torn nail is classified as Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a painful injury that requires medical attention. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 hours. Professional care is necessary to safely remove any dangling nail fragments, prevent infection, and provide pain relief. If the bleeding cannot be stopped at home within 10 to 15 minutes, you should seek immediate veterinary assistance.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of the injured paw can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows the triage staff to assess the angle of the break and the condition of the surrounding tissue before you even arrive at the clinic. This visual information helps them determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and prepares the staff to provide the most efficient treatment upon your arrival.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: You can use styptic powder, cornstarch, or even a bar of mild soap to plug the bleeding. Apply gentle pressure with a clean cloth for several minutes until the bleeding stops.

faqs.question: Can I trim the broken part of the nail myself?
faqs.answer: It is generally not recommended to trim the nail yourself if the quick is exposed. This is extremely painful and your cat may bite or scratch in response. A veterinarian can use local numbing or sedation if necessary.

faqs.question: What are the signs of infection in a torn nail?
faqs.answer: Watch for increased swelling of the toe, discharge or pus, a foul odor, or if your cat continues to limp or lick the area excessively after 24 hours.
